channel.title: Tony He Embedded Systems Lab
channel.description: Recent content on Tony He Embedded Systems Lab

item.title: Android Board Bring-up on Industrial SBCs
item.description: Android bring-up on an industrial SBC combines bootloader, kernel, hardware abstraction layers, display, input, storage, security policy, and system application behavior. Treating it as only an app problem usually leads to unstable products. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Android Display Debugging
item.description: Android display debugging is a full pipeline problem. The panel driver can be correct while SurfaceFlinger, hardware composer, rotation settings, or backlight control still produce a blank or unusable screen. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Android OTA and Recovery Strategy
item.description: A reliable Android product needs an update strategy before deployment. The system should recover from interrupted writes, bad application releases, power loss during update, and field devices with unreliable networks. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Android Peripheral Validation Checklist
item.description: Peripheral validation on Android should be repeatable and tied to board revision. A one-time manual test is not enough for a product that will ship with multiple hardware lots, displays, storage devices, and peripheral options. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Backlight and Brightness Control
item.description: Backlight design affects readability, power consumption, thermal load, and perceived product quality. A panel can show the correct image but still fail product testing because brightness control is noisy, inverted, or inconsistent. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Board Revision Tracking and Firmware Compatibility
item.description: Board revisions should be visible to software. Without revision tracking, one firmware image may behave differently across lots because GPIO assignments, display panels, PMIC settings, or peripheral options changed. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Buildroot Minimal Linux System
item.description: Buildroot is useful when a product needs a compact Linux system with predictable packages and a short build path. It is often a good fit for gateways, display controllers, appliance interfaces, and single-purpose industrial devices. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: CAN Bus Interface for Embedded Linux
item.description: CAN bus is widely used in vehicles, industrial automation, battery systems, and distributed control products. On Linux, a useful validation path combines hardware checks with SocketCAN tools. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Capacitive Touch Panel Integration
item.description: Capacitive touch integration is a combined hardware, driver, and UI problem. The controller may be visible on I2C while coordinates are rotated, interrupts are noisy, or the final application receives unusable input. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Custom Carrier Board Checklist
item.description: A custom carrier board changes both hardware validation and software assumptions. Even when the compute module is stable, the carrier can alter power sequencing, display wiring, storage, USB, Ethernet, GPIO, and mechanical access. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Device Tree Display and GPIO Review
item.description: Device tree review is one of the highest value steps in an embedded Linux project. It connects the schematic to the kernel driver model and often explains why a display stays blank, a reset line toggles at the wrong time, or a peripheral never probes. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: GPIO and Isolated I/O Design Notes
item.description: GPIO lines connect software to the physical world: buttons, relays, alarms, sensors, fixtures, and status outputs. In industrial products, GPIO design needs protection, clear ownership, and predictable boot states. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: HDMI Output Debugging
item.description: HDMI output debugging starts with link detection and display modes, then moves into color format, audio, hotplug behavior, and application rendering. It should be tested with more than one monitor. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: HMI Performance Testing on Low-Power Boards
item.description: HMI performance should be measured under real interaction, not only on an idle screen. Low-power boards can pass a demo and still fail when logs, network traffic, and touch events happen together. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Industrial Android Panel Kiosk Mode
item.description: Industrial Android panels usually run one controlled application instead of a general consumer tablet experience. Kiosk mode must handle boot launch, system navigation, updates, network loss, watchdog recovery, and restricted user access. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Industrial Control UI Structure
item.description:
Industrial control UI design is different from a marketing interface. Operators need fast scanning, stable status areas, clear alarms, predictable navigation, and safe confirmation flows. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

System context

In a real product, industrial Control UI Structure is rarely isolated from the rest of the system. It interacts with power sequencing, connector choices, firmware configuration, device tree or board configuration, factory tests, and the final enclosure. A feature that works on an open bench can still fail in production if the cable length changes, the product warms up, a different peripheral is installed, or the software image handles errors differently.

item.title: Industrial SBC Design Review
item.description: Industrial SBC design is a balance between compute module capability, I/O requirements, supply stability, thermal limits, lifecycle, and testability. The review should involve hardware, software, manufacturing, and service teams. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Lightweight HMI Update Strategy
item.description: HMI update design should be planned before the first field release. A broken UI can make a device appear dead even when Linux is still running, so rollback and recovery paths matter. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Linux Kernel Driver Debug Notes
item.description: Kernel driver debugging becomes easier when the driver, device tree, clocks, regulators, interrupts, and runtime logs are reviewed together. A failed probe is usually a structured dependency problem, not a random kernel behavior. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Long-Term Availability and BOM Control
item.description: Industrial products often need stable supply for years. BOM control is not only purchasing work; it affects drivers, calibration, mechanical fit, certifications, and field maintenance. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: LVDS Display Interface Notes
item.description: LVDS panels are common in industrial displays because they are stable, widely available, and suitable for moderate cable lengths. Integration depends on the correct timing, bit mapping, power sequence, and backlight behavior. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: LVGL on Embedded Linux
item.description: LVGL is a strong choice for compact HMIs that need predictable interaction without a heavy desktop stack. On Linux, the display backend, input path, redraw strategy, and asset sizes determine whether the UI feels responsive. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: MIPI DSI Display Integration Checklist
item.description: MIPI DSI display integration requires the digital display pipeline and the physical panel sequence to match. A framebuffer can render correctly while the panel remains black because reset, power rails, lane count, or initialization commands are wrong. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: PoE Power Module Integration
item.description: Power over Ethernet is useful when an embedded device needs both network connectivity and power from a single cable. It is common in access control terminals, industrial controllers, wall-mounted HMI panels, gateways, building automation nodes, and remote monitoring devices. For an embedded Linux product, PoE is not only a power input option. It affects Ethernet layout, thermal design, boot reliability, factory testing, and how the device behaves after a remote power cycle.

item.title: Production Testing for Embedded Boards
item.description: Production testing should prove that every shipped board has working hardware, correct firmware, readable identity, and traceable results. A good test plan is designed with the product, not added at the end. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Qt Embedded HMI Stack Notes
item.description: Qt is useful when an embedded HMI needs richer widgets, internationalization, complex layout, or a larger application structure. Platform plugin choice has a direct impact on graphics, input, and deployment. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: RGB TFT Panel Timing Review
item.description: Parallel RGB TFT panels look simple because they expose direct timing signals, but they can be sensitive to pixel clock, porch values, bus width, color order, and reset timing. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Rockchip BSP Bring-up Notes
item.description: Rockchip BSP work usually starts from a vendor tree that already boots on a reference board. The engineering task is to turn that baseline into a reliable product image for a specific carrier board, display, storage device, and production process. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: RS485 Interface Notes for Industrial Devices
item.description: RS485 remains common in industrial products because it supports differential signaling, long cable runs, and multi-drop communication. Reliable integration needs both electrical design and software timing. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: RTC Backup Power and Timekeeping
item.description: An RTC keeps system time when the main power supply is off. It is a small circuit, but it affects logs, certificates, scheduled actions, event records, and troubleshooting after a field device loses power. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Thermal Design for Enclosed SBCs
item.description: Fanless embedded devices are limited by enclosure design, ambient temperature, processor load, display brightness, and power conversion losses. Thermal work should start before layout and continue through product validation. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: Touch Calibration and UI Rotation
item.description:
Touch calibration and UI rotation must be handled consistently across kernel, compositor, toolkit, and application. Splitting rotation across layers often creates confusing behavior. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

System context

In a real product, touch Calibration and UI Rotation is rarely isolated from the rest of the system. It interacts with power sequencing, connector choices, firmware configuration, device tree or board configuration, factory tests, and the final enclosure. A feature that works on an open bench can still fail in production if the cable length changes, the product warms up, a different peripheral is installed, or the software image handles errors differently.

item.title: Touch Panel Integration on Android
item.description: Touch integration on Android starts in hardware but ends in user experience. A controller can probe successfully and still feel wrong if rotation, coordinate mapping, interrupt timing, or palm rejection is not validated in the final enclosure. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: U-Boot Debugging for Custom Boards
item.description: U-Boot is the first software layer that most board teams can observe and modify. A clean bootloader debug method separates power, strap, DRAM, storage, environment, and kernel handoff problems instead of treating every failure as a single boot issue. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.

item.title: UART Debug Interface for Embedded Boards
item.description:
UART is still the most important debug interface on embedded boards. Before Ethernet, display, USB, storage, Wi-Fi, or an application stack are stable, serial logs usually provide the first reliable view of bootloader and kernel behavior. A well-planned UART interface can save hours during first board bring-up, production testing, and field recovery.

For industrial SBCs and display panels, the debug UART is often treated as a small detail. In practice it is part of the product architecture. It determines how engineers see early boot failures, how a factory fixture records errors, and how a deployed device can be recovered when the main UI does not start.

item.title: Yocto Board Customization Workflow
item.description: Yocto is valuable when a product needs long-term maintainability, layered customization, package control, and reproducible images across board revisions. The challenge is not only building an image but keeping board changes organized over time. This page is written as an engineering note rather than a marketing overview. The goal is to identify the design choices, bring-up checks, validation evidence, and production risks that should be visible before a board or panel product is released.
